title: Tahdig
subtitle: Persian crispy-bottomed rice
excerpt: Iran's most coveted dish: basmati rice parboiled, then finished in a covered pot with butter or oil and a thin layer of lavash, pitta, or potato between the rice and the base, creating a golden, crunchy crust that is lifted out in a sheet and served as the centrepiece. Achieving a good tahdig is considered the mark of a skilled Persian cook.
sourceNotes: Tahdig (literally 'bottom of the pot') is a foundational technique in Persian cooking, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and in Sabrina Ghayour's 'Persiana' (2014). The technique of creating a crispy rice crust by cooking over indirect heat after parboiling is used in most Persian rice dishes. A non-stick pan with a tight-fitting lid is the most reliable tool; Persian cooks traditionally use a special pot and a cloth-wrapped lid.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tahdig must be eaten immediately — it loses its crunch quickly.

content.text: Persian rice is a two-stage process. The rice is first parboiled in abundantly salted boiling water until just cooked but still firm at the centre, then drained. The butter and water in the bottom of the pan create steam during the second cook; the heat is high at first to form the crust, then reduced to low to gently steam the rice above. The cloth wrapped around the lid (called a damdak) absorbs the steam and stops condensation from dripping back onto the rice. A clean tea towel works well.

The simplest tahdig uses just the rice itself as the crust. The pitta bread version (nan tahdig) is more forgiving for beginners — the pitta acts as a buffer between the rice and the pan, creating a crust that can be lifted off in one piece. The potato version (sib zamini tahdig) uses thin slices of potato at the base and is similarly reliable.

items.prepNote: rinsed until the water runs clear, soaked in salted water for 1 hour
steeped in 2 tbsp hot water for 10 minutes

content.text: Drain the soaked {{basmati-rice}}. Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil with 2 teaspoons of salt. Add the rice and boil for 5 to 6 minutes — the rice should be cooked on the outside but still slightly firm in the centre. Taste frequently: overcooked parboiled rice produces mush. Drain immediately through a colander.

In a large non-stick saucepan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium-high heat with 2 tablespoons of water. If using {{pitta-bread}}, lay it in the base to cover (trim to fit). Immediately pile the drained rice on top in a mound — do not pack it down. Use the handle of a wooden spoon to poke 4 to 5 holes down through the rice to help steam escape. Cover the lid with a clean tea towel and wrap tightly, then place on the pan.

Cook on medium-high heat for 5 minutes until steam begins to escape around the lid, then reduce to the lowest possible heat. Cook for 35 to 40 minutes. To check the crust: lift the lid briefly and listen for a slight sizzle, then replace. Remove from the heat and rest for 5 minutes with the lid on. If using {{saffron}} water, spoon it over the top of the rice before serving. To serve, invert onto a large plate, or scoop the fluffy rice out and break the crust into pieces separately.

items.cause: Heat too low or not enough butter and water
items.fix: The initial blast of medium-high heat for 5 minutes is what sets the crust. If your hob is weak, keep it at medium for 8 to 10 minutes. Ensure there is enough fat — a dry pan will not form a crust

items.symptom: Crust burns rather than turns golden
items.cause: Heat too high on the second stage
items.fix: After the initial 5 minutes, the heat must come down to the absolute minimum. A heat diffuser placed under the pot can help regulate this
Parboiling should leave the rice firm in the centre — not raw but with definite resistance. It finishes in the steam stage. Drain the moment it loses its raw core

items.symptom: Crust comes out in pieces rather than a whole sheet
items.cause: No pitta or thin potato layer, or pan not non-stick
items.fix: The pitta layer helps the crust release as one piece. A reliable non-stick pan is more important than any technique adjustment

content.text: Tahdig is the most important dish in the Iranian culinary canon. At any Persian dinner, the tahdig is the centrepiece: the crispy golden sheet inverted onto a platter and broken into pieces that are fought over by the guests. The quality of the tahdig is the measure of the cook: too pale, and the heat was insufficient; too dark, and it was burnt; the perfect tahdig is deep golden, crackling, and lifts cleanly from the pan. Persian rice cookery is built around this technique — the elaborate rice dishes of the Persian tradition (zereshk polo, baghali polo, adas polo) all use the same parboil-and-steam method, each with its own crust variation.
